data.title: ALPEK Still Profitable In Worst Part Of The Cycle, Trades At Low Valuation
data.description:
ALPEK (OTCPK:ALPKF) released 1Q25 earnings in late April, after part (but not all) of the tariffs chaos had lessened, and maintained a historically low guidance for FY25.

The company continued to stress that the US polyester, polypropylene, and styrofoam markets remain stressed and over-supplied, with margins that can be compared to the worst of the GFC. Despite this, the company's guidance still points to a level of EBITDA profitability that can cover CAPEX maintenance and growth needs, plus interest payments, and leave a net profit of $200 million.

Trading at about $1.1 billion, the stock offers a 5x earnings multiple on the worst part of the cycle in the US plastics market in decades. With a recovery in margins, Alpek could easily generate half of its market cap in one year of FCF. For this reason, and given the financial sturdiness of the company, I believe Alpek is a Strong Buy.

Challenging Q1 results

The company's Q1 results were the worst in a long time for margins and volumes. The company generated only $125 million in EBITDA, below the quarterly requirement to meet its FY25 guidance (more below).

The main reason was the deterioration of US competitiveness in polyester versus Asian imports because freight costs have receded versus last year. In addition, the company had operational challenges in the plants in Brazil and UK, and import competition in Argentina. Of these, the US factor is the most relevant. Reference margins in both Asia and China (from where Alpek margins are determined) decreased versus Q4.

The company still believes that margins will recover, especially in China, to moderately above current levels because they characterize the current Asian environment as highly stressed, even saying during the call that probably most Chinese producers are generating losses at Q1 margins.

Guidance was maintained despite tariff chaos

The company maintained the FY25 guidance released in 4Q24, which expects EBITDA of $625 million, $150 million in CAPEX ($50 million for maintenance, $100 million for growth), and $200 million in interest. This guidance is based on several assumptions about margins in each market, some of which were negated during Q1, so there is guidance risk.

For example, Asian and Chinese PET margins were expected to average $270 and $160 per ton (FY25 guidance announcement), respectively, but they sat at $278 and $140 for the quarter (1Q25 presentation). As mentioned, management believes the Chinese margins are unsustainable. For polypropylene, the expectation is margins of $0.14/lb, which has so far been proven true. Finally, the company expected containerized freight from Asia to decrease in cost, increasing the competitiveness of Asian imports. This has also proven true (unfortunately) with the China Containerized Freight Index making new lows. We need to wait for the data after the resumption of US-China trade.

Operational resiliency in historic low margins

One of the main positive points in my evaluation of Alpek is that the company is able to operate profitably, covering CAPEX and interest, in one of the worst market cycles for plastics in the US.

The polypropylene $0.14/lb margins were only lower during the GFC ($0.12/lb, mentioned during the call), and adjusted for inflation, they are even lower now. For PET, the Asian margins bottomed at $270 in 2020, and are today hovering barely above that (Institutional Presentation).

In this context, the company can produce at an EBITDA margin of 7.5% (1Q25 results). If the current margins have not been seen in years, it means that many producers in Asia and the US are underwater. Many probably cannot cover interest, not to mention CAPEX. Eventually, they will curtail production, and margins will rise.

Further, the company's end markets are 70% food and beverage and 20% consumer goods, or 90% in defensive industries (Institutional Presentation). Demand for these packages will not decrease significantly during a challenging economic period.

This all offers a level of downward protection for Alpek. Still, if producer margins were to decrease, I believe Alpek could still maintain a good level of profitability, as shown below for gross and EBITDA profits, when comparing with previous challenging margin periods (for example, 2012-2016 from the Institutional Presentation). The current breakeven rate for EBITDA is about $300 million ($200 million in interest plus $100 million in CAPEX), compared to an adjusted and guided level of $600 million.

Valuation is very attractive

If guidance is met, the company will generate $200 million in cash-based income (EBITDA minus CAPEX minus interest and taxes of 25%). Guidance could not be met if margins decrease even more, but I believe that situation is unlikely to remain, given the comparison with previous stressed margin levels in the plastics markets.

Compared to this, Alpek trades at a market cap of $1.1 billion, or barely above 5x FY25 guided earnings. That is, a company that is one of the lowest cost producers in the market (otherwise it would not be profitable under the worst margins in years), and that is financially solvent and has well-covered interest expenses, trades for 5 times the earnings generated in one of the worst markets in recent history.

This provides three potential sources for return. First, if things continue like this, eventually Alpek can pay dividends and materialize the earnings yield for shareholders. Second, Alpek's multiple can increase if the market realizes that the company will be able to survive the current downcycle and generate more earnings in the future. Finally, Alpek's earnings can improve if the plastics market cycle improves. During the pre-pandemic period, with more normal margins, the company consistently generated operating income in the order of $500 million, and even more than $1 billion during peak-cycle earnings in 2021/22.

For those reasons, I believe Alpek remains very undervalued, and increase the company's rating to a Strong Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SPYI May Be The Income ETF That Gets It Right
data.description:
In a recent analysis on S&P 500 Buy Write Index based ETFs, I had found significant shortcomings and recommended looking for ETFs that do not track the Buy Write very well. The Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (BATS:SPYI) could be that index which generates the alpha missing in the vast majority of ETFs based on option writing I have analyzed so far.

I start with my overall stance on option writing strategies as generally underperforming the underlying it works on, over long time frames. And therefore, you will find, in my earlier writings, calculations showing how you can invest directly in the underlying, match payouts periodically, and still end up with better ROI (including payouts). But there are other uses cases where such strategies may still be effective, which we will examine in greater detail in this analysis.

Underperformance Versus the Underlying

I have generally seen ETFs that are more aggressive in their option positioning (closer to the money) do better as they collect enough premium on wins to offset large losses that are bound to happen when positions turn negative. Now, SPYI does not give out exact specifics of its option positioning. One very distant measure of aggressive position is aggressive payouts (though that can be achieved simply through NAV erosion, which is also quite likely, as seen in several of these ETFs). SPYI pays out lesser than say a YieldMax income ETF. Whether that means less aggressive positioning of options and therefore worse performance over time, is what we will also see in this analysis. SPYI could be doing the option positioning right and paying out less aggressively, which is probably a better long term ploy.

Optimum underlying volatility is also a good predictor of options writing based ETF performance, but that works out more for single stock option writing ETFs than index-based ETFs (single stocks have sharper movements than a diversified index/portfolio).

SPYI recreates the S&P 500 index, sometimes with assumptions on material holdings and layers the underlying created with sold S&P 500 call spreads (meaning it will not reduce downside moves of the underlying very well, but will be able to capture some upside in case of rallies). SPYI has opportunity of capturing alpha in the construction of the underlying (cash positions) as well as the more obvious options positions. I am using SPY as a proxy for the underlying index and comparing it with the total return of SPYI (payouts + capital changes).

Overall returns expectedly are lower for SPYI (52% gains in SPY vs 37% gains including payouts for SPYI since inception), but if it shows a less volatile ownership of the underlying, it may still have some merits as an income plan. Remember, option writing strategies are expected to work best in sideways to slightly correcting cycles. The over-performance in these markets should be the reason to prefer the income plan over simply owning the underlying (in this case SPY) and simulating payouts matching the income plan. As seen, since inception, markets have been trending upwards and such markets often skew performance of income plans. Income plans should be all weather and then its underperformance vs. the underlying becomes acceptable.

Zooming in to what I saw as the most significant down market for S&P 500 since SPYI's inception, I see a $10K investment in SPY would have seen a 15.5% erosion compared to SPYI's total return being down by 13%. While this is not a huge lot of protection, it is somewhat reasonable compared to opportunity loss seen in the overall bull market since SPYI inception, which also played out for a longer tenure. A longer sideways market can show commensurate outperformance for SPYI. One of the reasons I had rejected JEPQ is its lack of downside protection, which is not the case for SPYI.

Conclusion - Although long-term investors can replicate payouts from SPY and still beat SPYI, there are some use cases for SPYI. Works well in sideways to downward markets. And in some way, provides a less volatile ownership experience of riding the SPY or S&P 500. This is at reduced management hassles of replicating income payouts from SPY and potentially better tax benefits (although income payouts can be structured to be long term capital gains and can be not much worse off, not significantly).

Income Specifics

SPYI does not payout aggressively. The payout yield of 12.52% will suit those investors who use SPYI to ride SPY with reduced volatility and derive income. Investors who look for higher aggressive payouts to de-risk themselves from the underlying and use the income to invest elsewhere for larger alpha, will not be disappointed, but the pace of returns still is well below some other income ETFs, such as the YieldMax ones. The YieldMax ETFs return 30-40% of the capital as payouts in a year, compared to the ~30% returns in the form of payouts for SPYI. There is a lot to like about the pacing of the payout for SPYI overall. It balances NAV erosion vs significant income generation very well in comparison to alternatives. Below is a chart showing how the NAV erodes faster in another aggressive payout ETF than SPYI.

Investment Advice

SPYI is certainly not a growth vehicle, and that is obviously not its stated objective. Income generation is decent and stable. The underlying NAV erosion is not as great as most income ETFs around. Performance is also acceptable with an income plan's limitations considered.

I would rate SPYI as a "Buy" for the specific use cases of income and riding S&P 500 with reduced volatility. You will stay invested for a longer time (less NAV erosion) and also grow your funds along with periodic income derived.

data.title: Carter's: Low Earnings Multiple Cannot Offset Its Negative Market Trends
data.description:
Carter's (NYSE:CRI) released 1Q25 earnings last month in the middle of the tariff war, and after recently changing its CEO. The company did not provide yearly guidance, but was confident for Q2 (April MTD).

Although most of the supply chain for Carter's had already been derisked, the US-China agreement announcement provides more certainty around the mid-term future of the US economy (avoiding the recession guaranteed by an embargo-type tariffs).

Still, I believe CRI is not an opportunity at current prices despite the improved macro outlook and the low optical multiple. The name trades at a 7.5x trailing earnings level, but has been losing sales and profitability ever since the post-pandemic. Unless this trend is reversed, a 7.5x multiple is not attractive. I prefer to wait and see how the new CEO sails the boat. I maintain my Hold on CRI.

Back to square one

When CRI released earnings for Q1 a month ago, it seemed as if those earnings could not tell anything about the future, because everything was about to change.

Fortunately, after the US-China agreement on tariffs from this weekend, it seems that very little is about to change, and that the results from last year and Q1 are important.

The company's exposure to China is very low anyway (2% according to the Q1 call), with 75% of production coming from Vietnam, Bangladesh, India, and Indonesia. However, the company was exposed to a US recession, a risk that is now moderated.

Management transition brings an outsider

The company's Chairman and CEO (30 years at Carter's and 15 as CEO) retired in March. Interestingly, he was replaced by an outsider (Doug Palladini) instead of by an internal manager. In principle, and without much more to judge the decision on, I would have preferred an insider.

Palladini comes from 3 years in consulting, and before that had been 17 years in Vans (VFC), where he reached brand President. From Doug's experience (Vans, Skullcandy), he seems to be more experienced in a different target customer (teenagers and young adults), but comes from a similarly sized brand.

He made very few comments during the Q1 call, which was handled by Carter's CFO.

Not great results with caveats

Q4 had been a good quarter for Carter's, as the company posted growing sales for the first time since 2021. The company's more aggressive pricing strategy was improving sales growth, albeit also impacting gross and operating margins.

Q1 was not the same, with comparables down 5% in US retail, International, and US wholesale. The company did not put the blame on a specific factor, but cited that Easter came later this year (end of April or Q2 instead of end of March for 2024). The impact was not quantified, but the company did mention that on a two-month basis, March-April was up 4% in comparables. March is 50% of Q1 sales, according to the call, so Easter can have a big impact.

In terms of profitability, the mix of lower gross margins (from more promotional activity) and sales deleverage continues to eat into profits. Operating income was down 37% for the quarter on an adjusted basis (adding back $6 million in CEO transition costs and $3 million in other restructuring).

Valuation and long-term challenges

Although the company did not provide guidance at the time, we can now expect 2025 to be more similar than disruptive compared to 2024. The tariffs are not so high as to generate a significant inflationary pressure on consumers, and both the supply chain and aggregate demand impacts of the chaotic April will eventually return to the mean.

Carter's now trades at a market cap of $1.25 billion, and the company generated $165 million in net income last year, or a 7.5x trailing earnings multiple.

For a company with good brand value, this should be attractive, but Carter's faces significant structural challenges. Without considering 4Q24, the company has been almost four years losing sales, which has translated into lower store profitability and much lower operating margins. This trend remained unabated as of 1Q25.

The company started to lean more heavily into proportionality in the second half of 2024, as a way to compete with more aggressive players in the market. I do not particularly like this strategy because price competition tends to lead to a race to the bottom, as shown for example by another (much more challenged) children's apparel retailer, Children's Place (PLCE).

Because the company had not gone through similar cycles in the past, it is very hard to forecast that margins and sales will return to a previous mean. The situation seems indicative of an underlying competitive trend.

With that in mind, and assuming only a 5% revenue decline, plus a 2pp operating margin decline, we would be talking of $120 million in net income for FY25, or already a more than 10x multiple.

For that reason, I believe the optically low earnings multiple on Carter's is justified. The situation would change with an improvement in sales, but most importantly in profitability. This may take a long time, but we should see some signals of progress in FY25.

Until then, I prefer to wait, and maintain a Hold rating on the name.

For Investor A shares: Total 0.79%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 0.79%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2026 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 4.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.44%; for Investor A shares: 0.69%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 0.29% (Institutional shares) and 0.24% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Income accrual canceled out negative price action as yields rose across the curve. The fund held a shorter duration position (lower sensitivity to interest rate movements) for most of the quarter. Higher current yield accrual benefits the fund when yield contributes a greater portion of the total return than price appreciation, with the quarter characterized by depreciation.
  • The fund had a quality bias. The cash and cash equivalent reserve was elevated at 11% due to the fund’s exposure to Treasury bills. Duration of six years was half a year below the benchmark, while 16.8% of securities were exposed to alternative minimum tax bonds. Leverage (borrowed capital) from tender option bonds was stable at 0.8%. The fund had overweight allocations to the corporate-backed municipal and transportation sectors.

Contributors

Detractors

The fund’s high income accrual, lower duration exposure, and higher-quality bias contributed to relative returns. Longer maturity securities underperformed shorter maturities as longer-term rates increased and the yield curve steepened. A robust new issue calendar allowed the managers to purchase attractively structured coupons at higher yields. The fund has become less negatively convex (where duration lengthens as rates rise), which should provide better price appreciation potential in future rate declines and caused duration to rise. The elevated cash and cash equivalent reserve was also beneficial.

Longer maturity, higher duration securities decreased in value as longer-term rates rose and the yield curve steepened. The market has become more concerned about longer maturities as the U.S. Treasury will debate how to finance larger deficits and roll over lower-yielding debt. High yield securities were a negative contributor as spreads for illiquid holdings widened, notably in the workforce housing and charter school sectors. Hedging weighed on relative performance, though it did not lead to a negative absolute total return.

Further insight

During the quarter, long-term interest rates rose, concerns about mutual fund cash flows increased, new issuance was robust, and questions were raised about the future tax treatment of the municipal asset class. Californian securities’ credit quality, while generally strong, is being debated at a local level as federal and state grants seem to be less forthcoming to cover budget shortfalls, and demographic trends work against the revenue portion of both state and local budgets. Consequently, Californian municipal bonds are relatively inexpensive at this point, especially for longer maturities. This yield relationship should be watched as the curve adjusts to future debt issuance forecasts.

★★★★★ Morningstar OverallTM

data.title: Accel Entertainment: A Quarter With Horsepower
data.description:
In my last write-up on Accel Entertainment (NYSE:ACEL), I mentioned how the stock was struggling, even though there was some upside on the horizon-especially tied to Fairmount Park, that century-old property in the St. Louis metro area that Accel, once just a VGT operator, went ahead and bought and renovated to a racino.

At $10, it really looked like a steal.

With fewer acquisitions in play and now owning a casino, Accel definitely felt undervalued. The VGT side of the business alone already delivers a great return on investment (mainly because it's so spread out, pumping capital into thousands of locations across multiple states and regions).

The market caught on fast.

Since my last call, the stock's up about 14.3% in just two months. Not too shabby! Over that same stretch, the S&P has only bounced back around 2.6% after the whole 'Trump Tariffs' scare.

With that kind of rally, the shares are now bumping up against my price target range of $12.50 to $13.50.

I must admit that this was faster than I initially expected, but I'm not going to complain.

Should we back up the truck and load up on more shares? Hold your horses (nice pun on Fairmount Park, don't you think?)-let's take a peek at the earnings first before we start popping champagne.

The year started off on a high note for Illinois' VGT darling. They reported EPS of $0.17, beating Wall St. estimates by $0.05. Meanwhile, top-line sales were $323.91 million-also beating Street estimates by $5.16 million.

This was the tenth straight double-beat for Accel (and that's if you exclude the last quarter that beat revenue only, with EPS in line). It's safe to say that Accel is still flying under the radar.

That $323.9 million in revenue represented 7.3% year-over-year growth-marking Accel's highest since going public half a decade ago.

We already expected a solid sales pop. After all, they scooped up Toucan Gaming late last year, bringing in 630 VGTs and a solid track record of growing down in the Southeast.

Accel's existing markets were also showing some strength, with momentum picking up across most segments. Breaking down VGT operations by state, we see the following:

  • Illinois: Daily check per VGT was $885, up 2.9% year over year;
  • Montana: Daily check per VGT was $610, up 2.7% year over year;
  • Nevada: Daily check per VGT was $802, down 5.3% year over year;
  • Louisiana: Daily check for VGT was $972;
  • Nebraska: Daily check per VGT was $263, up 12.9% year over year;
  • Georgia: Daily check per VGT was $145, growing 59.3% year-over-year.

With sales climbing, Accel managed to spread out its fixed costs better, pushing gross margin up to 30.98%-the best they've seen in two years, even if it's only a bit higher than usual.

With SG&A up about 100 bp year-over-year, this effect was more than offset, and EBIT margin declined from 8.56% in Q1 FY 2024 to 7.96% in the current quarter. In absolute terms, it remained stable year-over-year at $25.8 million. The same cannot be said for EPS. It was driven by lower interest charges on long-term debt and the impact of buybacks, coming in at $0.17-about 88% higher year-over-year.

Higher net income flowed nicely into operating cash flow, hitting $44.8 million-almost double what they did in the same quarter last year. And with no major acquisitions in sight, just some Capex spread across Fairmount, the Louisiana entry, and their core VGT biz (totaling $26.8 million), they ended the quarter with a solid $18 million in free cash flow.

Out of that $18 million in free cash flow, Accel used $14.9 million to pay down long-term debt and returned another $11.6 million to shareholders through buybacks.

Add in a few other financing odds and ends, and they ended the quarter with a $9.4 million cash deficit.

At the end of the quarter, they were left with $271.9 million in cash on hand and a net debt of $309 million (a Net Debt/EBITDA ratio of 1.83x, which is quite manageable).

Accel is only starting to show what kind of free cash flow it can really produce now that it's taking a breather from acquisitions and finally getting Fairmount Park up and running.

For FY 2025, Accel's expecting to spend $75 to $80 million in Capex-which, yeah, might cramp FCF a little and slow down buybacks short term. But once Phase 1 and 2 of Fairmount are done and Louisiana is off the ground, we're likely heading back to that sweet $40 to $45 million free cash flow range.

Just do the math: if OCF tops $140 million, you're easily looking at $90 to $100 million in FCF.

That's when the real shareholder returns start to heat up

From this point on, they will be able to gradually pay down long-term debt, kick off a dividend program, and ramp up buybacks.

That said, a hit from the discretionary spending slowdown this year would hit Accel in the Achilles heel. This is a year where we will see higher Capex and still reduced OCF because Louisiana is not yet finished and Fairmount is still in Phase 1.

But we saw a good proxy for Q2. And as I mentioned in my Boyd Gaming (BYD) write-up, we're not seeing any big pullbacks in casino spending. Far from it. The casual gamblers are still showing up and, more importantly, spending more. Retail's holding up well too.

According to Accel's management, there's been no noticeable shift in consumer behavior. Folks are still going out, playing, and spending like usual.

Fairmount Park: Open on Time, and Cheaper Than Budget

Fairmount Phase 1 opening got a double round of applause

Not just because it was ready in time for Derby Day, but also because it came in under budget. Can't ask for much more than that.

As a reminder, Phase 1 of Fairmount will see a temporary casino opening in the grandstands. The venue will feature 255 slot machines and a significantly enhanced food and beverage offering from the full-service restaurant Long Shots.

Even with this reduced footprint, the facility is expected to contribute approximately $8 to $7 million in EBITDA.

Phase 2 will not be complete until FY 2027. At that point, Accel will have 600 slot machines, 24 table games, and a new and improved FanDuel Sportsbook.

All of which will generate accretive annualized EBITDA of approximately $25 million.

All of this kicked off on April 18th, when the casino officially opened its doors (just 4.5 months after Accel wrapped up the Fairmount acquisition).

Just four days later, racing season got underway. And despite some hiccups - like scaled-back marketing and a few races getting hit (or even canceled) by bad weather - management reported solid traffic.

Still a 'Buy' After 15% Upside?

Like I mentioned in my last write-up, my price target for Accel is still in the $12.50 to $13.50 range.

That alone gives us about 7% to 15.5% upside. And if you throw in the buybacks management promised even before Phase 2 of Fairmount, we're looking at a little extra juice. Probably a few more percentage points on top of that.

But are stocks still cheap? And perhaps more importantly, is it time to bump up our earnings estimates? There's no denying that they've been beating Wall St. estimates for some time now.

Looking at the analyst consensus, they were expecting EPS of $0.86 for FY 2025, but they quickly raised it to $0.96 after the Q1 results were released.

So yes, it looks like we need to adjust our parameters a bit here.

If we take into account the projected EPS of $0.96 for FY 2025, we would have an implied P/E of 12.66x. Note that this is approximately 6% lower than what we had previously projected with the EPS of $0.86 (P/E of 13.58x).

But is this possible? I had expressed my reluctance to accept this year-over-year EPS growth, but I can be beaten here. If they are beating all the estimates, why wouldn't they beat these?

In any case, with this EPS and comparing it to the industry median (P/E of 15.32x) Accel could reach $14.70.

The logic of EV multiples also tells us that when we consider Accel's capital structure, the stock is still undervalued. Note that it trades at an EV/EBITDA of 6.34x, about 34% lower than the industry median. The same goes for its EV/EBIT, which is at 12.73x (13% lower than the industry).

Looking at it from this perspective, it may not be difficult for Accel shares to have room to reach the upper end of my expectation at $13.50, perhaps reaching as high as $14.

Note that even then, it would be around 10% lower than Wall St. estimates.

With those price targets in play, we're looking at an upside of around 15.6% to nearly 20%. Toss in the low-single-digit return from buybacks, and you're staring down a potential total return north of 20%. And let's not forget, Accel is doing all this with way less leverage than names like Inspired Entertainment (INSE) or even Light & Wonder (LNW).

That's a big plus in this environment.

That said, I reiterate my 'Buy' rating on Accel, with an increase in my PT to the $13.50 to $14 range.

data.title: Sitio Royalties: Strong Start To 2025 With Total Production Results
data.description:
Sitio Royalties (NYSE:STR) announced excellent Q1 2025 total production results, although its oil cut continued to decline. Sitio's oil production for Q1 2025 was still solid, but was only 2% above the midpoint of its full-year guidance compared to 6% for total production.

I am now modeling a scenario where Sitio meets its expectations for full-year oil production, but total production ends up near the high-end of expectations. At current strip of close to $64 WTI oil and $3.85 NYMEX natural gas, Sitio is now projected to generate $384 million (or $2.54 per share) in 2025 discretionary cash flow.

Sitio's return of capital to shareholders was $0.50 per share in Q1 2025, with roughly 70% of that going to dividends and 30% to share repurchases. The quarterly return of capital to shareholders during the rest of 2025 may be around 20% to 30% lower due to Q2 2025 to Q4 2025 oil prices potentially averaging around $10 lower than in Q1 2025.

I have trimmed my estimate of Sitio's value from $27 per share to a new range of approximately $24 to $25 per share. This reduction is due to a combination of relatively weak near-term oil prices and a $5 drop in my long-term oil price (to $70 WTI oil). This is partially offset by Sitio's stronger than expected non-oil production.

Q1 2025 Results

Sitio's production in Q1 2025 was quite good at 42,136 BOEPD, including 18,868 barrels per day of oil production. This was 6% above the midpoint of its full-year guidance for total production and 2% above the midpoint of its full-year guidance for oil production.

Sitio's oil cut has gone down to 45% in Q1 2025 versus 50% in 2024 and 47% for its full-year 2025 guidance, so that remains something to monitor. For Q1 2025 at least, Sitio's oil production did slightly better than expected while the declining oil cut meant significant outperformance with its total production levels.

Sitio reaffirmed its full-year production guidance, and I'd expect it to end up near the high-end of its total production guidance, but more towards the middle of its oil production guidance now.

Sitio generated $0.75 per share in discretionary cash flow in Q1 2025 and returned $0.50 per share to shareholders.

Of this, $0.35 per share was in the form of cash dividends and $0.15 per share (or $22 million) was in the form of share repurchases. Sitio repurchased approximately 1.1 million shares in Q1 2025 at an average price of $20.19 per share.

It also spent around $8 million repurchasing shares during Q2 2025 (up to May 2), repurchasing nearly 0.5 million shares at an average price of $16.17 per share.

Revised 2025 Outlook

I am now modeling Sitio's 2025 production at 41,000 BOEPD (45% oil). This would involve total production close to the high-end of its full-year guidance along with oil production around the midpoint of its guidance. This does not include the impact of any potential future acquisitions.

Sitio's production during the last three quarters of 2025 would only need to average approximately 3% lower than its Q1 2025 production to reach those numbers.

At the current strip of near $64 WTI oil and $3.85 NYMEX natural gas, I project Sitio to generate $569 million in 2025 revenues after hedges.

Sitio now expects around $23 million in cash income taxes for 2025. It reduced its expectations around 2025 cash taxes by approximately $5 million compared to its original guidance due to the weaker commodity prices.

At current strip prices, Sitio is projected to generate $384 million in 2025 discretionary cash flow now, or approximately $2.54 per share.

Sitio may return around $1.65 per share to shareholders during 2025 in the form of dividends and share repurchases. This is based on a continuing 65% payout ratio.

Sitio returned $0.50 per share to shareholders for Q1 2025, so it may average around $0.35 to $0.40 per share in quarterly returns related to the remainder of 2025.

This is around 15% to 20% lower than Q1 2025 due to the lower oil prices, plus my assumption (which may be conservative) for -3% production during the rest of the year (compared to Q1 2025).

Estimated Valuation

I had estimated Sitio's value at $27 per share at long-term (after 2025) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

I've now changed my estimate to $24 to $25 per share based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

The lower long-term oil price as well as weak near-term oil prices cuts into Sitio's estimated value by several dollars. This is partially offset by its relatively strong non-oil production. While oil production is significantly more valuable on a per BOE basis, the strong non-oil production still adds a few cents per share to Sitio's discretionary cash flow.

Conclusion

Sitio reported strong Q1 2025 production results with oil production slightly above expectations, while its total production ended up around 6% above its full-year guidance midpoint.

Sitio maintained its full-year guidance, but I can see it potentially doing better than guidance for total production at least. I am now modeling Sitio's 2025 production at 41,000 BOEPD (45% oil), with $2.54 per share in discretionary cash flow at current strip.

Mostly due to lower oil prices, Sitio's quarterly discretionary cash flow may average around $0.60 per share during the last three quarters of 2025 compared to $0.75 per share in Q1 2025.

I now value Sitio at $24 to $25 per share, factoring in lower discretionary cash flow in the near-term and $70 WTI oil after 2026.

data.title: Applied Optoelectronics Reports Improved Q1 2025 Financial Performance As It Ramps Up Production
data.description:
Applied Optoelectronics (NASDAQ:AAOI) reported Q1-2025 earnings last week and shared its outlook for Q2-2025. The company has reported higher revenue for the last two quarters due to increased demand for its data center and CATV products. AOI forecasts revenue growth through 2025 as it ramps up US and Asian manufacturing.

The stock price has increased 82% over the last twelve months. It is down 22% since I covered it last March on Seeking Alpha. A new deal between US and China concerning tariffs has caused a rally in the market and AOI’s stock price is rising. It is up 24% today on the news. The company says that tariffs do not affect its overall financial performance.

I continue my rating of a Buy, although it comes at a higher risk. Operating costs and R&D remain high for the company. Other analysts on Seeking Alpha have rated the company as a Sell due to high operating costs and low profit margins. Margins are improving for the company and revenue should remain elevated from the company’s current increase in manufacturing.

Q1-2025 Results

The company reported revenue of $99.9 million, representing a 145% increase YoY and a less than 1% decrease QoQ. Gross profit was $30.5 million or a 30% margin, compared to $7.6 million gross profit YoY or an 18% margin. Gross profit for the previous quarter was $28.7 million, or a 28% margin. The company reported a net loss of $9.2 million for the quarter, which shows an improvement YoY and QoQ.

Total operating expenses for the quarter were $39.8 million, representing a historic high for the company. Of the total, $17.8 million was used for R&D, a 52% increase YoY. Total debt is $46.1 million, which does not include convertible debt. The company is spending more to expand its manufacturing capabilities.

The company reported $64 million in revenue from its CATV segment, a 600% increase YoY and a 24% increase QoQ. The increase was due to shipments of the company’s 1.8 gigahertz amplifiers to one of its US cable MSO customers. Revenue from CATV products set a record for the company. A large North American cable operator adopted the company’s quantum bandwidth networking products in different geographic areas.

The company reported $32 million in revenue from its data center products, representing an 11% increase YoY and a 28% decrease QoQ. The company foresees increasing demand for its data center products as data centers develop their next-gen infrastructure with AI in mind. Data centers will require more speed and need better optics and switches.

The company saw a rise in orders for its 100G products, likely due to tariff fears. The company was limited in its 100G products due to supply constraints. The company expects increased revenues from 100G products during Q3 after it replenishes its supply.

Telecom segment revenues were $2.9 million, representing a 29% increase YoY and a 17% decrease QoQ.

Overall, 65% of revenue was from CATV products, 32% was from data center products, and 3% was from FTTH, telecom, and other products. For Q1-2025, 97% of the company’s revenue came from its top 10 customers. One customer in the company’s CATV market represented more than 10% of the company’s revenue. That customer contributed 64% of revenue. Another customer in the company’s data center market, representing more than 10% of revenue, contributed 27% of revenue.

CapEx for Q1-2025 was $30.5 million. The money was used for ramping up manufacturing in the US and Asia. The company expects CapEx to remain elevated. The company has partially offset its expenditures with at-the-market offerings. The company has raised $98 million so far in ATM offerings.

For Q1-2025, tariffs had no impact on the company’s financial performance. The company does not foresee any impact from tariffs on Q2.

Q2-2025 Outlook

For Q2-2025, the company expects revenue between $100 million and $110 million. A net loss between $4.8 million and $1.7 million. Market consensus estimates Q2 revenues at $104.96 million, an increase YoY and QoQ. AOI foresees a QoQ decrease in revenue from CATV business segment products and an increase in revenue from data center products. The company reports in August.

AOI reports that operating costs, particularly R&D and G&A, will continue to increase through 2025. Total operating costs may increase as high at $40 million per quarter. The company expects between $120 million and $150 million in total CapEx for 2025. AOI forecasts net income for 2025 fiscal year and foresees profit margins to continue to increase through 2025.

The company is expanding production capacity for its 800G optical transceivers at its Houston, Texas manufacturing plant. The company will be able to produce 40K transceivers per month. The company uses automation for its manufacturing and can scale up production. The company hopes to produce over 200K per month by 2026.

AOI will be shipping products this summer from the new expanded capabilities. The company has increased domestic manufacture to meet new requirements of its US data center customers. The company expects higher 800G product sales beginning in Q3 when its domestic manufacturing will be in full swing.

The company expanded its manufacturing capabilities at its Taiwan site. It has increased production of its 100G, 400G, and 800G data center transceiver and CATV products. AOI has three manufacturing sites, one in Houston, another in Ningbo, China, and another one in Taipei, Taiwan.

Historical Financial Performance, Valuation, and Risk

AOI has reported increasing revenues over the last eight quarters. Profits and margins have also increased. The company is achieving its goal of $100 million per quarter. The new manufacturing output may guarantee these elevated revenues in the future. There is a risk that the company will not maintain this level of sales through 2025.

Operating expenses are historically high. AOI has seen increased expenses due to expansion of manufacturing capabilities and research for product development. The company is always updating its products for customer demand. The rising costs become a risk in the long term if the company has a few low earnings reports.

AOI has continually reported a net loss per quarter. Q1-2025 net loss shows the greatest improvement. The company does not report free cash flow. These factors also make the company’s stock a high risk. The company expects net income over 2025, but it is a fine balance between expenditures versus gross profit. R&D is necessary for continued success. Other CapEx will decrease after ramping efforts are completed.

The company’s assets ($644.7 million) outweigh its liabilities ($335.7 million). Cash and ST investments have increased with ATM offerings. The company is at low risk of poor financial performance. The biggest question is whether heightened demand for its products will continue.

The company’s stock is undervalued based on its forward multipliers, like NTM EV / Revenues. The stock price trades 3x compared to its book value. The stock trades 50% below its 52-week high of $44.50. Its current price seems undervalued compared to the company’s revenue growth and recent profit margins. Investors have positive sentiment on this stock based on its high daily trading volume and momentum. A Buy rating still comes at a high risk since the price has seen a lot of volatility over the last twelve months.

Comparison to Peers

I will provide more coverage in the future on other companies, which outfit data center infrastructure and CATV products. Companies like AXT (AXTI), CommScope (COMM), and NETGEAR (NTGR) offer similar or related products to the same sector and markets. Each company does not offer the exact same items or pieces of infrastructure, so they are hard to compare.

AXT (AXTI) provides specialty non-silicon wafers used in fiber optics and data centers. The company has robust demand for its bespoke products, but its financials are plagued by low revenues, low profits, and net loss. Like AOI, this company makes products specifically for the customer’s needs and usage. There is a constant dance between R&D and product launch. AXT has an interesting business strategy, including its own raw minerals provider in China. I have covered this company on Seeking Alpha.

I have not covered CommScope nor NETGEAR. CommScope provided similar services as AOI. It manufactures products for data center infrastructure, including fiber optics. CommScope reports over $1 billion per quarter in revenue. It reports net income and free cash flow. Its stock price is up 433% over the last twelve months. The company’s liabilities outweigh its assets, and it reports between $6 billion and $7 billion in debt. This company has some risks associated with it.

NETGEAR supplies products for data centers and other markets. The company consistently reports high revenues and gross profit, but operates at a net loss. The company has shown free cash flow over the last eight quarters, with a few exceptions. The company’s balance sheet is strong, and its stock is up 143% over the last twelve months. Each of these companies has reported an increase in demand for data center products.

Currently, I am watching AOI, AXT, and CommScope.

Conclusion

AOI reported strong financial performance for Q1-2025 and shared an optimistic outlook for the rest of 2025. The company expects $100 million in revenue per quarter to become its new normal. AOI provides it products to very large “hyperscaling” clients in the US. We hear that they have contracts with Microsoft (MSFT) and for data centers associated with Amazon (AMZN). The company does not share any details about its clients nor its specific role in outfitting their data centers. AOI foresees continued increase in demand for its products and the company has increased manufacturing capabilities to meet this demand. There is risk that the company’s expectations for 2025 do not come to fruition. Greater stock market volatility may beat the price down. Investors should be cautious and watch for developments from the company. I continue my rating of a Buy.

data.title: UniCredit: Lower Interest Rates Yet To Hit Profitability
data.description:
The profits continue to roll in for Italian banking giant UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY) (OTCPK:UNCFF). Having earned a 17.7% return on tangible equity last year, Milan-based UniCredit has followed that up with an exceptional 22% ROTE in respect of the first quarter of 2025. While I expected its earnings to be resilient in my last update, that is an eye-popping result in the face of lower interest rates. Unsurprisingly, this has been a boon for its shares. The ADSs have even managed to return another ~30% since my last update in February, taking their total return to around 185% since I first covered it back in 2023.

Of course, multiple expansion accounts for a large part of these returns. UniCredit's superb operational performance has earned it a price-to-tangible-book-value ratio of around 1.70x, up from roughly 0.85x when I opened on it. While investors may balk at paying that for a European bank, the strength of UniCredit's earnings should keep its price/earnings ratio below 10.0x. Alongside its well-capitalized balance sheet and associated dividend/buyback potential, I remain bullish.

Lower Rates Yet To Hit Profitability

UniCredit's first quarter results were a clean sweep, with the bank comfortably outperforming consensus on revenue, costs, and net income. The latter landed at €2.72 billion. This was around 9% higher than the year-ago period, roughly 19% ahead of consensus, and mapped to an exceptionally strong annualized ROTE of 22%, thus making UniCredit one of the most profitable banks I cover.

UniCredit's ROTE looks even more remarkable when you consider two additional points. Firstly, it continues to sit on a significant amount of surplus capital. If UniCredit's Common Equity Tier 1 ratio was in line with management's 13% target (it was 16.1% at the end of 1Q25), its ROTE would have been 370bps higher.

Secondly, UniCredit should theoretically be one of the more rate sensitive European banks I cover. That is largely a reflection of is incredibly attractive domestic funding base. In Italy, UniCredit earned 425bps on its loans last quarter, yet it paid just 36bps on its deposits. Thanks to higher interest rates and a good stock of variable rate loans, its average loan yield in Italy is over 250bps higher than it was three years ago, driving almost 100% growth in domestic net interest revenue in that time. Italy accounted for roughly half of UniCredit's pre-tax earnings last quarter, making this market a key driver of its results.

NIR is now declining slightly from its cycle highs, falling around 2.9% year-on-year last quarter to €3.47 billion. In the bank's core Eurozone markets (Italy, Germany, and Austria), NIR fell between 4% and 8%, offset slightly by volume-driven growth in its periphery central & eastern European businesses.

In the face of this inevitable slowdown in NIR growth, I have been looking for a couple of things from UniCredit. Firstly, for the bank to make good progress in growing non-interest revenue, thus alleviating any top-line pressure from lower interest rates. And secondly, for strong cost control to provide further support to pre-provision earnings.

1Q25 was excellent in this respect. Non-interest-revenue was €3.08 billion, up 10% year-on-year and 20% above consensus. While volatile trading income drove much of the beat, fee revenue was also strong, rising 8.2% year-on-year to €2.30 billion. This was almost 6% ahead of consensus. As a result, and despite lower NIR, overall revenue grew 2.8% year-on-year to €6.55 billion, beating by around 8%.

On costs, while UniCredit has no doubt seen upward pressure from inflation and the need to invest in technology, it has also been able to realize efficiency gains by rationalizing its legacy brick-and-mortar operations, thereby keeping total expense growth muted. This was also a standout feature of last quarter's results, with operating expenses landing at €2.32 billion, up just 0.6% year-on-year and €30 million below consensus. Given cost growth was less than revenue growth, UniCredit managed to a realize an 80bps year-on-year improvement in it cost/income ratio, which is a best-in-class 35.4%.

Credit quality was also a help. Last quarter was a particularly strong one for provisioning, with UniCredit booking just €83 million in loan loss provisions (equal to ~8bps of customer lending). While that powered some of the bottom line beat (consensus had LLPs at €144 million), this shouldn't take distract from the bank's pre-provision performance. At €4.23 billion, pre-provision profit grew 4% year-on-year, beating by a comfortable 14%. Besides, while the provisioning line did help pad UniCredit's profitability last quarter, it would have been strong even with more normalized credit charges. LLPs in line with management's initial 2025 target (15bps), for example, would still have left ROTE above 20%.

Asset quality remains resilient, with non-performing exposure down 10bps year-on-year to 2.6% (flat sequentially). For all the talk of tariffs, management now expects a more benign cost of risk this year than initially guided for, aided by the bank's relatively large stock (around €1.7 billion) of macro overlays. These overlay provisions essentially aim to reflect new risks not captured by existing credit loss modeling. Either these risks emerge, in which case the bank is provisioned for them (at least to some extent), or they don't, in which case they will be released back through the income statement, supporting earnings.

Shares Good For A Large Premium

UniCredit shares trade for €55.19 apiece in Milan as I type ($30.87 per ADS), equal to roughly 1.70x 1Q25 tangible book value per share. While that is rich territory for a European bank stock, UniCredit's 20%-plus ROTE warrants a large premium to book value. Indeed, if we were to annualize 1Q25 net income, the stock's price/earnings ratio would only be around 7.9x. This would rise to just 9.2x based on management's initial 2025 net income target (roughly €9.3 billion).

Then there's the balance sheet to consider. UniCredit has €46.3 billion in CET1 capital and €287 billion in risk-weighted assets, mapping to a CET1 capital ratio of 16.1%. That means there's around €9 billion in surplus capital relative to management's 13% goal. This is allowing UniCredit to return everything it earns via dividends and buybacks. For example, UniCredit announced €9 billion in capital returns in respect of fiscal 2024 results, split around 60% in favor of buybacks. Management intends for annual 2025–2027 distributions to exceed this level, implying an ongoing 10%-plus shareholder yield.

Conclusion

UniCredit has started its fiscal 2025 with a knockout set of first quarter results. Not only did the bank deliver a comprehensive double beat, but it earned a stellar 22% return on tangible equity in the face of decline in interest rates. While its ROTE was padded a little by lower provisioning, the bank's pre-provision performance was also excellent.

As for the shares, UniCredit's recent results have won it a valuation more commensurate with its earnings power. While multiple expansion won't be the tailwind it was, UniCredit's profitability and strong balance sheet can still support attractive returns via dividends and share buybacks. Accordingly, I remain bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Peloton: Gym Expansion And Cost Cuts Anchor This Rebound Story (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm watching the rapid bounce back in the S&P 500 with a large degree of apprehension. Even though China tariffs have been put on hold, there's still a dark cloud over the U.S. economy, which we can already see via a pullback in consumer spending (retail and restaurant companies have reported vastly weaker same-store sales growth this earnings quarter; travel companies have cited weaker demand and airlines are cutting capacity). Against that backdrop, I continue to favor smaller-cap stocks that have independent catalysts to outperform the broader market.

Peloton (NASDAQ:PTON) is one such play. Though the fitness equipment maker has fallen sharply from grace, the company's new direction under new CEO Peter Stern, the former head of Apple Fitness+, is promising. Shares of Peloton have sunk ~20% since the start of the year even though the company has shored up its profitability, creating what I believe to be a well-timed buying opportunity.

I last wrote a neutral, cautious opinion on Peloton in February, when the stock was still trading in the mid-$8s. Since then, a number of factors have swung in the positive direction for this company. For one, the company reported excellent Q3 results that finally showed stabilization in subscriber counts, alongside a healthy rise in gross margins and overall profitability that led Peloton to lift its full-year adjusted EBITDA expectations. Second, the company has quantified and confirmed the de minimis impact of tariffs (a $5 million negative FCF headwind in fiscal Q4) on its business, covering only the aluminum used in Peloton and Precor-branded equipment as well as immaterial apparel purchases from China. With this in mind, I've turned cautiously optimistic on Peloton, and I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

To me, these are the core reasons that make me more sanguine on Peloton's prospects:

  • Enterprise business development opportunities. It's true that Peloton may be losing momentum as a direct-to-consumer brand, as evidenced by its falling hardware sales. However, the company has recently elevated a new Chief Commercial Officer to oversee a number of initiatives, including selling Pelotons directly to gyms and hotels. The company recently released a new collection of on-demand workouts across 2,400 Hilton locations.
  • Running, strength, and men. Despite recent subscriber declines, the company has seen meaningful increases in non-rowing workouts, especially running and strength, which is also bringing in a larger male demographic to Peloton's user base. In my view, Peloton's strategy to lean into these more comprehensive workout offerings can broaden its customer base and give the company a chance to reset its brand.
  • Committed to profitability. Peloton has slashed expenses dramatically over the past few years, leading to meaningful adjusted EBITDA growth as well as slimming down its debt profile. In my view, the company's stabilizing top-line trends on top of its improving profitability and slimming debt load makes the company far more appealing to a potential private equity buyer.

By far, valuation remains one of the most compelling reasons to invest in this stock. At current share prices just under $7, Peloton trades at a market cap of $2.70 billion. After we net off the $914.3 million of cash and $1.50 billion of total debt on Peloton's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $3.29 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25 (which ends in June 2025), Peloton has made several positive tweaks to its outlook. After outperforming on paid subscribers in Q3, the company lifted its year-end paid subscription guidance to 2.77-2.79 million members, 20k higher at the low end versus its prior outlook. It also raised its adjusted EBITDA outlook to a $340 million midpoint (14% margin), 5% higher versus the midpoint of the prior $300-$350 million range even after incorporating tariff impacts in Q4.

This positions Peloton's valuation at just 9.7x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA, for a business that is currently growing adjusted EBITDA at a >50% y/y pace. In my view, the company has plenty of levers for double-digit adjusted EBITDA expansion ahead in FY26, including a higher degree of commercial gym/hotel sales, subscriber stabilization, and flow-through from the recent trend of higher hardware gross margins. The single-digit adjusted EBITDA multiple, to me, is a great value-oriented entry point: and it also increases the likelihood of private equity interest in the name (where possible buyers have been rumored to circle Peloton since mid-2024).

While Peloton is certainly not without its risks, the stock has now fallen to very buyable levels. Take advantage of the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Peloton's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q3 (March quarter) earnings summary is shown below:

Peloton's revenue declined -13% y/y to $624.0 million, driven in particular by a -27% y/y decide in hardware revenue but coming in ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $621.7 million. Meanwhile, we continue to see signs of encouragement on the subscription side, where the company added total paid Connected Fitness subscribers in the quarter to end with 2.88 million subs, breaking a recent trend of declines. We note that the company's guidance for Q4/FY25 still calls for as slight seasonal decline in Q4 (as historically the company has noted more members churn during the warmer summer months), but we note that the fact that the company is emphasizing different types of workouts (running, strength) may help Peloton to break from prior seasonal norms. We note as well that average churn rates improved 20bps to 1.2%, versus 1.4% in Q2.

Per CEO Peter Stern's remarks on the Q3 earnings call on member engagement:

We observed 5% year-over-year growth in running workouts and 11% growth in walking workouts. Engagement with newer features such as Pace Targets on our treadmill continued to improve. In Q3, over 80% of Tread users taking a running workout used Pace Targets, up from just under 60% last quarter.

We see further opportunities to keep winning cardio by delivering innovative software, hardware and even more engaging content. We'll share more about our innovation roadmap later this calendar year.

Turning to holistic wellness. In Q3, we saw a higher mix of workout time in strength disciplines quarter-over-quarter and year-over-year. We also delivered new kettlebell content in late February and nearly 70,000 members completed kettlebell workouts by the end of the quarter. Beyond strength, meditation classes taken increased 7% year-over-year in the third quarter."

The company noted as well that marketing outreach to members at risk of churn helped to improve subscriber retention rates above the company's initial expectations. And yet even despite this, the company still managed to heavily slash spending, particularly in sales and marketing, which declined -38% y/y to $106 million in the third quarter. The company also cut down R&D spending by -22% y/y, driven by headcount reductions.

On top of a 780bps y/y boost in gross margins (driven by a higher hardware gross margin, plus a more favorable revenue mix toward subscriptions), Peloton was able to boost adjusted EBITDA to $89.4 million (a 14.3% margin), which grew 53% sequentially and was meaningfully better than a near-breakeven $5.8 million adjusted EBITDA in the year-ago Q3.

We note as well that Peloton has jumped to a ~$100 million FCF run rate over the past few quarters, which it has directed into a hefty deleveraging of its net debt position:

Going forward, I expect that Peloton's increasing mix of sales toward enterprise locations (gyms, hotels, universities) may help the company to stabilize its overall churn rates and build a base for continued adjusted EBITDA margin improvement.

Risks and key takeaways

It probably goes without saying that Peloton's aggressive attempts at a turnaround comes laden with a number of risks, which include:

  • Competition amid weaker consumer spending. Peloton is still positioning itself as the premium fitness brand, and amid macroeconomic headwinds, customers may choose to buy cheaper alternatives like NordicTrack.
  • Memberships have yet to return to meaningful growth. Though Peloton appears to have stabilized its near-term churn, it still has a ways to go until it can show meaningful member growth again, which outside a potential takeover offer is the main path back to improved valuation multiples.

Still, I believe these risks are more than encapsulated into Peloton's bargain-basement <10x forward adjusted EBITDA multiples. In my view, it's time to stake a small position here and bank on a broader rebound.
